Approach 1: NuScale Power Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in current terms.

For NuScale Power, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $460.39 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections show free cash flow staying negative in the next few years, then reaching $103.66 million by 2030, with further projected figures continuing to rise after that based on Simply Wall St estimates.

Pulling all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $7.96 per share. Compared with the recent market price of $11.71, this implies the shares are about 47.1% above the model’s estimate of fair value. This points to a rich valuation on this cash flow view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: NuScale Power Price vs Book

For companies where profits are limited or still developing, the price to book ratio is often more useful than earnings based metrics because it compares the share price with the value of net assets on the balance sheet.

In general, the higher the expected growth and the lower the perceived risk, the higher the multiple investors may be willing to pay, while slower growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower, more conservative multiple.

NuScale Power currently trades on a P/B of 3.19x. That sits above the Electrical industry average P/B of 2.77x and below the peer group average of 10.55x, so simple comparisons send a mixed signal about how stretched the valuation is relative to others.

data.title: Is BNP Paribas (ENXTPA:BNP) Pricing In Its Strong Multi‑Year Share Price Run?
data.description:

  • The stock has returned 1.5% over the last 7 days, 7.6% over the last 30 days, 11.5% year to date, 40.5% over 1 year, 92.6% over 3 years and 156.7% over 5 years. This gives useful context before looking at what the valuation signals show.

Commentary has also highlighted how investors are weighing sector specific risks and capital return policies when assessing bank stocks like BNP Paribas.

Find out why BNP Paribas's 40.5% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: BNP Paribas Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank can generate over and above the return that equity investors typically require, and then links that to the value of its shares.

For BNP Paribas, the model works off a book value of €104.44 per share and a stable earnings per share estimate of €13.23, based on weighted future Return on Equity inputs from 15 analysts. The average Return on Equity used in the model is 11.14%, compared with a calculated cost of equity of €8.77 per share. That gap feeds into an excess return of €4.47 per share, which is then applied to a stable book value estimate of €118.77 per share, itself based on book value estimates from 9 analysts.

Running these inputs through the Excess Returns framework gives an intrinsic value estimate of €206.26 per share. Against the current share price of €91.37, this implies a 55.7% discount, which indicates that the shares screen as materially undervalued on this method.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: BNP Paribas Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like BNP Paribas, the P/E ratio is a practical yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay for current earnings, given the company’s profile.

What counts as a “normal” P/E ratio tends to reflect two things: how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how much risk investors see in those earnings. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often go with higher P/E ratios, while slower growth or higher risk usually go with lower ones.

BNP Paribas currently trades on a P/E of 8.73x. That sits below the Banks industry average P/E of 11.31x and also below the peer group average of 13.24x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for BNP Paribas is 8.53x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E “should” be after considering factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, industry and market cap. Because it blends these company specific inputs, it can be more tailored than a simple comparison to industry or peer averages.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 8.53x with the actual P/E of 8.73x suggests the shares are slightly above that level, but the gap is small.

Olympus (TSE:7733)

Overview: Olympus is a long established Japanese medtech company that builds endoscopy and surgical imaging systems used in hospitals worldwide, from gastrointestinal and respiratory procedures to urology, gynecology and ear, nose and throat surgery, alongside related devices, software and servicing.

Operations: Olympus generates the bulk of its revenue from Gastrointestinal Solutions at ¥630,099m and Surgical & Interventional Solutions at ¥357,254m, with sales spread across North America, Europe, Japan, China and the rest of Asia and Oceania.

data.title: Progressive (PGR) Combined Ratio Of 87.4% Tests Bearish Margin Narratives
data.description:
Progressive (PGR) opened 2026 with solid momentum, coming off Q4 2025 revenue of about US$22.7b and basic EPS of US$5.03, backed by trailing 12 month figures of US$87.6b in revenue and EPS of US$19.29, alongside year over year earnings growth of 33.6%. Over the past reported periods, revenue has moved from roughly US$20.3b in Q4 2024 to US$22.7b in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS ranged between about US$3.99 and US$5.42. This sets up a story where margin trends and underwriting performance take center stage for investors assessing the latest results.

See our full analysis for Progressive.

With the headline numbers set, the next step is to weigh them against the prevailing narratives about Progressive's growth, risk profile, and earnings outlook to see which views hold up and which start to look outdated.

Margins Backed By An 87.4% Combined Ratio

  • Over the last 12 months, Progressive reported a combined ratio of 87.4% alongside a net margin of 12.9%, compared with 11.2% the prior year, which means underwriting and overall profitability have both been running at relatively healthy levels for a major insurer.
  • What bullish investors highlight is that this combination of a sub 90% combined ratio and 33.6% earnings growth is exactly what they point to when arguing Progressive’s pricing and telematics capabilities can support strong profitability. The bearish narrative, however, counters that climate and cost inflation risks could still pressure that 87.4% combined ratio if claims volatility rises from here.
    • The bullish side leans on Progressive’s use of data and rapid rate adjustments to argue that underwriting margins can stay attractive even when costs move, which lines up with the improved net margin implied by the latest 12.9% figure.
    • Skeptics focus on the risk section that flags rising repair and healthcare costs plus weather events as threats to loss ratios, using those points to argue that today’s 87.4% combined ratio might sit above where they would like to see it in a more stressed claims environment.

Bulls argue this margin profile could underpin further upside if pricing power holds, while skeptics are watching closely for any cracks in underwriting quality before they give the all clear on the profit story. 🐂 Progressive Bull Case

Valuation Gap Versus DCF Fair Value

  • Progressive trades around US$201 per share while the provided DCF fair value is US$458.65, so the stock is referenced as sitting roughly 56% below that DCF fair value even though the trailing P/E of 10.4x is a bit higher than the 9.0x peer average and lower than the 11.6x US Insurance industry average.
  • Critics highlight that, despite this apparent discount to DCF fair value, the same dataset shows analysts expect earnings to decline by about 0.9% per year over the next three years, which they argue makes it harder to justify Progressive trading well above peer P/E levels for long.
    • The bearish narrative leans on that forecast earnings decline and the mention of margin compression risk to argue the current 10.4x P/E might already be fair if profitability normalizes, even with the large headline gap to DCF fair value.
    • At the same time, the 33.6% trailing earnings growth and 12.9% net margin are used by more optimistic investors as evidence that the market might be underweighting recent execution, so there is a clear tension between the backward looking strength and the forward looking caution baked into those forecasts.

Skeptics warn that the combination of a forecast earnings decline and higher than peer P/E could limit how much of that DCF gap actually closes, making it important for you to stress test whether the current US$201 price already reflects more cautious profit expectations. 🐻 Progressive Bear Case

Revenue Growth Trails Market, Earnings Outpace

  • Over the last year, Progressive’s revenue grew 6.7% while earnings grew 33.6%, so profit growth has outpaced the top line even though the 6.7% revenue growth trails the 10.8% US market benchmark cited in the data.
  • Consensus narrative notes that Progressive’s technology driven distribution and telematics give it an edge in winning and retaining customers. However, the fact that revenue is growing slower than the broader market while margins improve suggests the recent 33.6% earnings growth has leaned more on profitability than on breakaway premium growth.
    • On one hand, the stronger margin profile and 12.9% net margin support the idea that analytics and pricing discipline are adding value, which is consistent with the consensus view that Progressive operates efficiently.
    • On the other hand, the 6.7% revenue growth versus a 10.8% benchmark shows why some investors may question how durable the current earnings pace is if volume growth stays below that wider market reference.

Approach 1: Sterling Infrastructure Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes projected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth in current dollars.

For Sterling Infrastructure, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow (FCF) is reported at about $355.7 million. Analyst and model projections used here point to FCF of $433.9 million in 2026 and $484 million by 2030, all in $. Estimates beyond the first few years are extrapolated using Simply Wall St's 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model.

data.title: SAP BlueSnap Integration Ties Cloud ERP To Stickier Payments Story
data.description:
• SAP (XTRA:SAP) has introduced a native direct to core payment integration with BlueSnap for SAP Cloud ERP and S/4HANA.
  • The new integration removes the need for third party payment connectors and is available as a certified solution for SAP enterprise customers.
  • The launch is aimed at simplifying global payment processing, reconciliation, and compliance for multinational operations.

SAP, trading at €146.54, is moving to tighten the link between its core ERP platforms and global payment flows at a time when many enterprises are looking to simplify cross border commerce. The stock shows a mixed performance profile, with a 31.3% return over 3 years and 36.3% over 5 years, set against a 27.4% decline year to date and a 36.4% decline over 1 year. For investors watching XTRA:SAP, this kind of product move is directly aligned with SAP’s core strength of supporting complex global operations.

For enterprise clients already standardised on SAP, a certified, direct connection to BlueSnap could help reduce reliance on custom payment builds and multiple intermediaries. Looking ahead, the key things to watch are customer adoption of the integration, any visible impact on payment efficiency metrics, and how SAP positions similar partnerships across other financial workflows.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for SAP by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on SAP.

We've flagged 0 risks for SAP. See which could impact your investment.

This BlueSnap integration sits squarely in SAP’s push to make its cloud ERP offerings more of a one stop operating hub for global enterprises. By plugging a payment provider directly into SAP Cloud ERP and S/4HANA, customers can run payment processing, reconciliation, fraud checks, and tokenized transactions without layering separate connectors on top. For companies with multi entity, multi currency structures, that kind of simplification can be attractive when comparing SAP against suites from Oracle or Microsoft, or against more modular setups that stitch together several vendors.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The SAP Narrative

  • The move supports the narrative that SAP wants deeper integration into customer workflows, with payments data flowing directly into core ERP and potentially reinforcing higher recurring revenue from its cloud platforms.
  • It also tests the narrative’s assumption that SAP can keep execution tight while restructuring for AI, because complex, real time payment flows leave less room for integration issues or downtime.
  • The partnership model with a payment specialist like BlueSnap, including PCI compliant processing and local acquiring coverage, is not deeply detailed in the narrative and may add nuance to how SAP leans on partners rather than building everything in house.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for SAP to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • Execution risk if large multinational customers face disruption during migration from legacy payment service providers or from existing third party connectors.
  • Competitive pressure if integrated offerings from Oracle, Microsoft, or payment players like Adyen and Stripe are perceived as more flexible or easier to implement.
  • Potential for tighter payment and ERP integration to make SAP stickier within large accounts, supporting longer contract terms and higher upsell potential.
  • The PCI compliant, tokenization ready setup with fraud protection and multi entity support could appeal to complex global customers that value a single account structure for payments.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how often SAP or BlueSnap reference customer wins that explicitly use this direct to core integration, any commentary on improved authorization rates or lower cross border costs, and whether SAP repeats this pattern across other financial workflows. Also watch how often payments are highlighted during SAP’s earnings calls or events like Sapphire, especially in comparison with cloud ERP and AI messaging, because that will signal how central these payment partnerships are becoming in SAP’s wider product story.

data.title: A Look At Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group’s Valuation As XINYUE Gains Third Rare Disease Approval In China
data.description:
Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group (SEHK:3692) has secured NMPA approval for XINYUE’s third indication, targeting adult patients with generalized myasthenia gravis, and expanding its rare autoimmune disease footprint supported by positive Phase III MINT trial data.

See our latest analysis for Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group.

The XINYUE approval lands at a time when the share price has climbed to HK$39.4, with a 30 day share price return of 21.6% and a 1 year total shareholder return of 82.34%. This suggests recent momentum on top of already strong longer term gains.

If this kind of rare disease news has your attention, it can be worth widening your watchlist with other healthcare names using our screener of 125 healthcare AI stocks

With Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group now trading at HK$39.4 and still showing both an intrinsic and analyst target discount, investors may be wondering whether there is more upside left or if the market is already pricing in future growth.

Advertisement

Preferred P/E of 37.4x, Is it justified?

On a P/E of 37.4x at HK$39.4, Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group sits well above both its own fair value estimate and the peer group, which points to a rich valuation.

The P/E multiple compares the share price to earnings per share and is a quick way to see how much you are paying for each unit of profit. For a pharmaceutical company with meaningful earnings, a higher P/E often reflects investor expectations for future growth, product approvals or sustained profitability.

In Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group's case, several factors point to the market paying up. Earnings grew 27.1% over the past year, ahead of its 5 year average of 17.4% and ahead of the Hong Kong Pharmaceuticals industry, which saw a 1.2% decline. Profit margins are currently 37%, above last year's 35.7%, and earnings growth over the past five years has averaged 17.4% per year. The SWS DCF model also indicates the shares trade about 22.8% below an estimated future cash flow value of HK$51.04, suggesting cash flow based valuation is more supportive than the current P/E might imply. However, the estimated fair P/E of 29x sits well below the current 37.4x. This highlights how far sentiment has moved above what the regression based fair ratio suggests could be a more grounded level if expectations cool.

Compared with peers, the premium is even starker. Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group's 37.4x P/E is higher than the Hong Kong Pharmaceuticals industry average of 15.2x and above the peer average of 24.3x. That gap indicates the market is assigning the company a premium multiple that is more than double the sector level and clearly above what the SWS fair ratio model indicates.

Explore the SWS fair ratio for Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group

Result: Price-to-Earnings of 37.4x (OVERVALUED)

However, recent 90 day share price weakness and the premium 37.4x P/E leave the story vulnerable if drug approvals, licensing partnerships, or earnings execution disappoint.

Another View: DCF Says the Shares Look Cheap

While the 37.4x P/E suggests Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group is priced for high expectations, the SWS DCF model points the other way. With the shares at HK$39.4 versus an estimated future cash flow value of HK$51.04, the model indicates roughly a 22.8% undervaluation. Which signal deserves more weight?
